--

In a recent viral post, NVIDIA CEO Jensen Huang’s words landed like a gauntlet: “Nobody writes prompts anymore. The new job is to write and handle loops.” This isn’t just another AI hype cycle — it’s a fundamental shift in how we build, deploy, and interact with intelligent systems. Here’s why it matters for 2026 and beyond.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

A few days ago, tech commentator Anatoli Kopadze posted a striking clip (with a 53-minute deep dive attached) featuring Jensen Huang declaring the end of the prompt era. The post quickly gained millions of views, striking a chord because it crystallized something many practitioners have felt building for months: prompt engineering, once the hottest skill in AI, is rapidly being eclipsed by something more powerful and systemic.
The quote, drawn from recent comments by Huang (including an Associated Press interview), points to a broader evolution. As models grow more capable at reasoning, tool use, and long-horizon planning, the bottleneck is no longer “how do I phrase this question perfectly?” It’s “how do I design an autonomous process that keeps working, evaluating, and improving until the goal is achieved — with minimal babysitting?”
That process is what Huang and others are now calling loop engineering (or agentic loops, self-improving workflows, etc.).
From One-Shot Prompts to Closed-Loop Systems
Prompt engineering dominated 2023 — early 2025 for good reason. It was accessible, powerful for many tasks, and let non-experts extract impressive results from base models. You crafted instructions, added examples, used chain-of-thought, and iterated manually until the output was good enough.
But it has hard limits:

• Brittleness — Small changes in context or model version break results.

• One-shot or few-shot nature — Most prompts are stateless; the model doesn’t inherently “learn” or refine across attempts unless you manually feed back outputs.

• Human-in-the-loop intensity — Complex work still requires constant prompting, reviewing, and re-prompting.

• Poor scalability for reliability — For research, coding, analysis, or operations, a single good answer often isn’t enough. You need verification, iteration, and grounding.
Loop engineering flips this. Instead of optimizing a single prompt, you design a cycle:
1. Perceive / Retrieve — The agent gathers information (search, documents, tools, memory).
2. Reason / Plan — It breaks down the goal, forms hypotheses or steps.
3. Act / Execute — It uses tools, writes code, calls APIs, generates drafts.
4. Evaluate / Critique — It scores output against explicit criteria, checks facts, runs tests, or uses another model as judge.
5. Reflect & Iterate — Failure modes or improvements feed back into the next cycle.
6. Repeat until success criteria are met or max iterations reached.
The human’s job shifts from “write the perfect prompt” to “define the goal, success metrics, guardrails, and high-level architecture of the loop,” then monitor and steer at a higher level. The AI does the heavy lifting of iteration at machine speed.
Huang described future AI as moving from “guessing” to “research”: systems that search, compare multiple versions, evaluate truthfulness or quality, chase references, and refine — exactly the loop pattern.
Why This Shift Is Happening Now (and Why NVIDIA Cares)
Several converging forces make 2026 the inflection year:
• Model capabilities — Stronger reasoning, native tool calling, longer context, and better self-critique make reliable loops feasible.
• Test-time compute & scaling — More inference budget spent on iterative refinement (rather than just pre-training) delivers outsized gains. This is “less guessing, more research” in practice.
• Enterprise needs — Companies want reliable automation, not chatty copilots. Loops turn AI into persistent workers or teams that run for hours/days with oversight, not constant intervention.
• Token economics & infrastructure — Loops are token-heavy by design (many iterations, parallel exploration). NVIDIA’s inference platforms, optimizations, and ecosystem are built precisely to make this scale efficiently and cost-effectively.
This aligns with NVIDIA’s broader vision — from cuOpt-style agentic optimization to physical AI and robotics, where closed-loop control (perceive-act-observe) is foundational. The same principles that make a robot navigate the world make an AI agent research a topic, debug code, or run a multi-step workflow.
Andrew Ng has echoed similar sentiments: self-improving loops are the next frontier, and prompting as the primary interface is on its way out within months for many tasks. Anthropic engineers report heavy internal use of these patterns, with dedicated courses now available for free.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

What Loop Engineering Looks Like in Practice
• Self-improving coding agents — Plan → write code → run tests → analyze failures → fix → repeat.
• Research agents — Query → search + retrieve → synthesize + critique sources → identify gaps → iterate.
• Multi-agent teams — One agent plans, another executes, a third reviews, a fourth synthesizes — with shared memory and orchestration.
• Long-running autonomous workflows — Goal set once; the loop runs across sessions, checkpoints progress, escalates only on blockers.
Tools and platforms are evolving fast to support this: Claude’s Projects and Skills, various agent frameworks, orchestration layers, and emerging “team mode” or persistent workspace products. The common thread is statefulness, explicit evaluation, and iteration built into the architecture rather than bolted on via clever prompting.
The New Skills (and Mindset)
PThe prompt engineer of 2024 becomes the loop architect of 2026. Key capabilities include:
• Defining crisp, measurable objectives and stopping criteria.
• Designing feedback mechanisms (self-critique, tool-based verification, human review points).
• Building guardrails, escalation paths, and observability.
• Choosing the right decomposition and orchestration pattern (single agent vs. multi-agent, synchronous vs. asynchronous loops).
• Monitoring system behavior and iteratively improving the loop itself.
This is systems thinking applied to AI — less like writing a query, more like designing a small autonomous organization or process.
What This Means for Builders, Enterprises, and the Industry
For individual developers and creators: The highest-leverage work is no longer perfecting every output. It’s building reusable loop templates and agent teams that compound over time.
For enterprises: This is the difference between AI pilots that require constant human oversight and production systems that deliver reliable throughput. It changes the ROI math dramatically.
For NVIDIA and the infra layer: More loops = more inference demand, executed in parallel and over longer durations. The companies that optimize for this (efficient serving, memory management, orchestration at scale) win.
The viral X post and the 53-minute discussion it references are symptoms of a broader awakening. Prompt engineering was the on-ramp. Loop engineering is the highway.
The Road Ahead
Jensen Huang’s comment isn’t dismissing prompting entirely — it’s contextual. For quick interactions, prompts still work great. But for anything ambitious, complex, or requiring reliability, the unit of work is shifting from the prompt to the loop.
Those who master designing, testing, and steering these loops will build the next generation of AI-native products, workflows, and companies. The prompt was how we talked to AI. The loop is how AI works for us — autonomously, iteratively, and at scale.
The rest of 2026 will belong to the loop engineers.
What are you building with agentic loops or self-improving workflows right now? Have you noticed the shift from prompt crafting to process design in your own work? Share your experiences in the comments — I’d love to hear what’s working (or not) in practice.
Thanks to Anatoli Kopadze for surfacing the clip and sparking wider discussion. For deeper dives, check out free resources from Anthropic on loop engineering with Claude, Google’s agentic engineering materials, and Andrew Ng’s recent commentary on self-improving agents.
